dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for breathing problems at --Address Redacted--, Apt #109 on 2019-09-20.
Hi, my name is Jordan, and I'm an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I've been struggling to breathe and coughing up some brown phlegm for about a week now.
Ma'am, can you open your eyes for me?
So you know what's going on and can open your eyes-great.
Can you tell me what day it is today?
Saturday, I think.
That's fine. Can you squeeze my fingers for me?
Sure, here.
Your mouth and nose look clear. Can you speak in full sentences comfortably?
Yeah, I can talk but I just feel like I can't get enough air.
Ma'am, we're going to check your breathing now.
Your respiratory rate is 25. That's a bit fast, but not in full distress.
It sounds like your breathing is wheezy, and there are some crackles in your lungs.
Let's put on the pulse oximeter to check your oxygen saturation.
Your blood oxygen is at 68%. That's low. We're going to give you more oxygen than what you're already using.
We are going to put on a nasal cannula with 3 liters of oxygen.
Your blood pressure is 157 over 69. That's a bit elevated but within non-critical range.
Your skin looks normal in color, warm, and dry. I can see a strong pulse at your wrist.
Your heart is beating 108 beats a minute - pretty fast.
Let me check your blood sugar quickly. It might be high-can I draw blood now?
Yeah, go ahead.
Okay, your blood sugar is 271 mg/dL. Pretty high, but you mention it's low for you.
To better help you, can you tell me a bit more? Let's start-what symptoms are you experiencing right now?
I'm having trouble breathing-it feels like a 9 out of 10. Also coughing up brownish phlegm.
Have you used any breathing treatments or medication for this?
I used my nebulizer a few times today, but nothing helped.
In terms of this difficulty breathing, how would you describe it? Maybe rate it from 0 to 10.
Probably a 9. My back hurts too-it feels like pain from an old injury that acts up when I'm sick.
We're placing you on a cardiac monitor to check your heart rhythm-this is standard.
Your heart rhythm is sinus tachycardia-characterized by a fast, regular rate.
We'll do a 4-lead EKG next. Is that acceptable?
Go ahead.
The 4-lead shows sinus tachycardia with marked sinus arrhythmia and a short PR interval.
I want to give you albuterol and atrovent via a nebulizer to help get your breathing better.
We will start your nebulizer therapy now and make sure your oxygen flows smoothly during the treatment.
We're going to perform a two-provider cross-check before giving you the medication.
We have administered 5 mg of albuterol and 0.5 mg of atrovent via nebulizer mask at 8 LPM of oxygen.
Following protocol 3-11 for respiratory distress with COPD or reactive airway, we're going to reassess after interventions.
How are you feeling with the nebulizer? Can you rate your shortness of breath now?
A bit better. Still feels heavy, but I can speak full sentences.
Let me take your vitals again. Your blood oxygen is at 99%-that's a big improvement.
Your blood pressure is 95 over 43 now. That's lower-everything still feels okay?
Your breathing difficulty is now a 6 on the 10-point scale, right?
Yeah, it's eased up some. Doesn't feel like it's getting worse now.
I'm going to check your skin now-nothing out of the ordinary, right?
No, it feels normal.
How are you feeling while we shine this light into your eyes?
Bright, but I can keep my eyes open.
Your mental status is.alert and oriented-could you tell me who you are and where we are?
My name is ... --Name Redacted--... and we're in Apt #109.
What year is it right now?
Perfect. You're alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation-AOx4.
I'm going to move each of your arms and legs-are you following?
Sure.
Your range of motion in all extremities is intact, and your gait is stable when moving.
I need to run a 12-lead EKG-may I place the pads on your chest?
Go ahead.
The 12-lead EKG shows possible evidence of lateral ischemia, marked ST and T wave abnormalities, and signs of subendocardial injury.
There's also strong evidence of left ventricular hypertrophy with high QRS voltages.
I will set up an IV line in your arm-this will prepare us for any medication as needed.
Your blood pressure is now 141/63. That's within a non-critical range.
We'll keep the IV a saline lock for now. Can you let me clean and access your arm quickly?
Now, I'll slowly administer 10 mg of dexamethasone intravenously-this should help with inflammation.
Great, let's try another nebulizer therapy and reassess afterward.
We will start a second albuterol nebulizer at 8 LPM oxygen. But I noticed half the treatment spilled, so we'll continue with the remainder now.
Let's take another reading. Your respiratory rate is now 27. Earlier, you had 25 and now it's 20-mild tachypnea but improving overall.
Your heart rhythm is now a regular sinus rhythm-no signs of arrhythmia for now.
Your oxygen saturation stays at 99%-good sign of effective treatment.
Tell me-have you had episodes like this before, and did you go to the emergency room?
I did, but was told it was just the flu and that I should stop overdoing it.
I see. Any allergies not already listed? Does that affect asthma risk at all?
Seasonal allergies for sure, but no medications I take today are something I'm allergic to. And no, no asthma diagnosis I know of-COPD only.
I remember you mentioned pain earlier. On a scale of 1 to 10, how much was it?
It started at 9 and is now a 6. It was primarily chest, but the back pain is from an old injury that flares up when I'm sick.
What medications are you currently using, and are you on any new ones lately?
I use albuterol, my blood pressure pill, and an insulin shot for diabetes. I also used nebulizers at home today before you came.
I see your complex medical history-hypertension, COPD, diabetes-insulin dependent, hyperlipidemia, anxiety, bipolar disorder, and a history of pancreatitis and a past car accident.
That about sums it up. My blood pressure pill is also a fluid pill.

You mentioned swelling in your feet for the past couple of days-is that something your PCP addressed? That can sometimes indicate fluid retention or heart issues-have you had any symptoms like needing to sit up at night or chest tightness?

My PCP said it may be from the fluid pill. I haven't had chest tightness from fluid buildup overnight, but I have had some difficulty breathing when laying down recently. I think I was told before that it might be from my legs holding fluid-nothing more serious. My doctors never diagnosed me with CHF or pulmonary edema-just swelling. My COPD is worse, definitely, and I think I have obstructive sleep apnea-been on a CPAP on and off. It helps sometimes, but not all the time. Even when I sleep through the night with it, I wake up still fatigued, especially worse when I'm sick now, which is why I went off it recently. I never had asthma or croup in childhood. Definitely wheeze now, more like COPD maybe-exacerbation this time, for sure.
You said your pain feels like an old injury-was it from a car accident?
Yes, about 20 years ago. It's flared up when I get sick.
You were using a CPAP in the past-have you been using it recently?
No, I've been on and off with it.
We've taken all immediate care steps, and I'd like to get you to a hospital now.
Is there a hospital you prefer, or would you like us to take you to the nearest one?
Please take me to [COMPANY_NAME].
I will assist you with standing and help position you in a position of comfort on the stretcher.
We will make sure you are as comfortable as possible.
You will be covered with a sheet and stabilized with five point restraints before we load you into the ambulance.
Your condition is stable, and we've done everything we can. The field impression includes Respiratory-COPD Exacerbation and Infectious-Bronchitis-Acute.
Our EKGs were unremarkable for STEMI, and your oxygen levels have stabilized quite well.
We are transporting you cold to increase monitoring at the hospital.
I will go over all vital signs, our intervention details, and our medications to the hospital team.
The patient is fully conscious with a GCS of 15 (E4; V5; M6), indicating she is alert and oriented.
The patient reported a history of anxiety and major depression-important for hospital consideration.
The patient has known allergies to tramadol, morphine, naltrexone, ibuprofen, and ketorolac.
The patient reported a history of intubation.
The patient denies history of pulmonary edema or congestive heart failure.
And that's our report. Let's roll-be seen soon at the hospital, ma'am.
We also measured a tympanic temperature of 100.1°F and monitored for end-tidal CO2 at 70-80 mmHg.
Your heart rate is now at 82-this is a decrease compared to earlier.
Your respiratory rate has dropped to 20-still mild tachypnea, but an improvement.
Your extremities are moving without restriction and demonstrate intact range of motion at this time.
